Document Title: Pseudo-Temporal Modeling with 2D CNNs for Framewise Surgical Gesture Segmentation in Robot-Assisted Surgery
Authors: D. Haro-Mendoza; G. A. Castro Vazquez; A. Leonardo Campos Carreras; A. J. Diaz Urias; V. J. Gonzalez-Villela; M. Cedillo-Hernandez; R. E. Arevalo-Ancona
Author Affiliations: Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Biomedical Systems Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Dept. of Mechatronics Engineering, Faculty of Engineering, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; SEPI ESIME Culhuacán, Instituto Politécnico Nacional, Mexico City, Mexico; SEPI ESIME Culhuacán, Instituto Politécnico Nacional, Mexico City, Mexico
Publication Title: 2025 6th International Conference on Communications, Information, Electronic and Energy Systems (CIEES)
Abstract: Surgical procedures can be decomposed into elementary units (tasks, surgemes, and dexemes) that represent the building blocks of surgical performance. Segmenting gestures at this level is crucial for objective skill assessment and for understanding how simple movements are concatenated to form complete procedures, ultimately supporting the development of training and evaluation systems. In this work, we present a pseudo-temporal modeling approach for surgical gesture segmentation using a 2D ResNet-50 convolutional network trained exclusively on RGB frames. During training, each video was divided into eight equal segments, which were used to feed the network and generate gesture-level probabilities. To optimize learning, an adaptive Exponential Moving Average (EMA) mechanism was applied to stabilize weight updates and reinforce poorly performing gesture classes. At inference, complete surgical videos were processed with an overlapping sliding window of eight frames, where each frame could be evaluated multiple times in different contexts. Frame-level probabilities were averaged across overlapping windows and refined through a temporal mode filter, producing stable and coherent gesture sequences. The method was validated on the JIGSAWS dataset, which includes the Suturing, Knot Tying, and Needle Passing tasks. Results showed reliable recognition across all procedures: Suturing achieved 82.26%, Knot Tying 91.97%, and Needle Passing 79.35%. Training required approximately one hour, and inference on a complete video of 2950 frames was performed in about 45 seconds, also generating a labeled output video. By relying solely on 2D convolutions and RGB input, the approach achieves accurate and efficient segmentation with low computational cost, making it suitable for surgical training environments with limited resources and applicable in near real-time scenarios.
Author Keywords: surgical gesture segmentation;pseudo-temporal modeling;2d convolutional neural networks;resnet 50;exponential moving average
IEEE Terms: Training;Adaptation models;Accuracy;Computational modeling;Surgery;Needles;Real-time systems;Windows;Convolutional neural networks;Videos

Document Title: Passive Fault-Tolerant Control via Sliding-Mode-Based Lyapunov Redesign
Authors: M. A. Estrada; L. Fridman; J. A. Moreno
Author Affiliations: Department of Robotics and Control, Engineering Faculty, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), Mexico City, Mexico; Department of Robotics and Control, Engineering Faculty, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), Mexico City, Mexico; Instituto de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), Mexico City, Mexico
Publication Title: IEEE Transactions on Automatic Control
Abstract: This article presents an innovative framework for passive fault-tolerant control (FTC) based on the Lyapunov redesign utilizing sliding mode controllers (SMC). The article introduces three schemes for passive FTC design. First, it advocates employing the Lyapunov redesign in the FTC design with the unit SMC, resulting in a generation of a discontinuous control signal. The article establishes novel conditions for system insensibility concerning the norm of actuator faults. To address chattering and reduce energy consumption, an alternative FTC design is proposed, leveraging a multivariable version of the super-twisting algorithm, which generates a continuous signal. Additionally, a novel methodology for gain selection is developed to ensure increased tolerance to actuator faults. Finally, a new multivariable version of the Lipschitz algorithm, based on the classical twisting controller, is formulated and applied to passive FTC through the Lyapunov redesign, promising a reduced level of chattering and energy consumption compared to the super-twisting algorithm. The efficacy of the proposed FTC schemes is illustrated through simulation examples, highlighting both their advantages and potential limitations in practical applications.

IEEE Terms: Actuators;Perturbation methods;Uncertainty;Lyapunov methods;Trajectory;Fault tolerant systems;Fault tolerance

UPCC-SIC., Barcelona, Spain; Instituto de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico
Publication Title: 2025 6th International Conference on Control and Fault-Tolerant Systems (SysTol)
Abstract: This paper proposes a method to estimate both the friction factor and the roughness coefficient of the pipes in a water distribution network (WDN). Pressure head data is used for the training of a Long-Short Term Memory (LSTM) neural network to predict the pressure head at all of the other nodes in the network, as well as the flow rate through the pipes. When all the data is predicted, the estimated pressure head drops, and the flow rate for the entire water distribution network is used to compute both the friction factor and the flow rate. The proposed method is tested using the well-known Hanoi DMA (District Metered Area) as a case study. Satisfactory results are obtained when comparing the predicted friction factor and roughness coefficient against the expected values.
IEEE Terms: Training;Friction;Neural networks;Estimation;Process control;Distribution networks;Predictive models;Calibration;Sensors;Long short term memory

Document Title: A Reasoning System for Graded Modal Logic
Authors: R. López-Villafán; E. Bárcenas
Author Affiliations: IIMAS: Posgrado en Ciencia e lngeniería de la Computación, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico; Facultad de Ingenería: Dpto. de Computación, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Mexico City, Mexico
Publication Title: 2024 12th International Conference in Software Engineering Research and Innovation (CONISOFT)
Abstract: In this paper, we propose a deduction system for the modal logic $\mathbf{K}$ with graded modalities, which is obtained by adding numerical constraints to the modal operators. Graded modalities can be seen as a generalization of traditional modal operators since they include numerical bounds in the modalities, thus enabling the formal treatment of more detailed expressions, such as “there are at least $n+1$ individuals satisfying a certain property”, where $n$ is a natural number. This kind of properties commonly arise in contexts such as concurrent systems: “if at least 3 processors are free, then proceed to compute the next task”, or epistemic logic:”if more than 2 people know $P$, then $P$ is known”. The system is based on the notion of tree-hypersequents. We show the system is correct, i.e. sound and complete.
Author Keywords: graded modalities;proof theory;hypersequents;automated reasoning
IEEE Terms: Technological innovation;Interpolation;Program processors;Computational modeling;Syntactics;Cognition;Numerical models;Logic;Iterative methods;Formal verification

Abstract: The first steps towards integrating autonomous monitoring, probabilistic forecasting, reachability analysis, and adaptive sampling for the Gulf of Mexico were demonstrated in real-time during the collaborative Mini-Adaptive Sampling Test Run (MASTR) ocean experiment, which took place from February to April 2024. The emphasis of this contribution is on the use of the MIT Multidisciplinary Simulation, Estimation, and Assimilation Systems (MSEAS) including Error Subspace Statistical Estimation (ESSE) large-ensemble forecasting and path planning systems to predict ocean fields and uncertainties, forecast reachable sets and optimal paths for gliders, and guide sampling aircraft and ocean vehicles toward the most informative observations. Deterministic and probabilistic ocean forecasts are exemplified and linked to the variability of the Loop Current (LC) and LC Eddies, demonstrating predictive skill by real-time comparisons to independent data. Risk forecasts in terms of probabilities of currents exceeding 1.5 kt were provided. The most informative sampling patterns for Remote Ocean Current Imaging System (ROCIS) flights were forecast using mutual information between surface currents and density anomaly. Finally, we guided four underwater gliders using probabilistic reachability and path-planning forecasts.

Abstract: As part of the Mini-Adaptive Sampling Test Run (MASTR) experiment in the Gulf of Mexico (GoM) region from February to April 2024, we demonstrated real-time deterministic and probabilistic reachability analysis and time-optimal path planning to guide a fleet of four ocean gliders. The governing differential equations for reachability analysis and time-optimal path planning were numerically integrated in real-time and forced by currents from our large-ensemble ocean forecasts. We illustrate the real-time deterministic and probabilistic forward reachability analyses, reachability and path planning for glider pickups, time-optimal path planning for gliders in distress, and planning of future glider deployments. Results show that the actual paths of gliders were contained within our reachable set forecasts and in accord with the dynamic reachability fronts. Our time-optimal headings and paths also predicted real glider motions, even for longer-range predictions of weeks to a month duration. Reachability and time-optimal path planning forecasts were successfully employed for glider recovery. They also enabled exploring options for future glider deployments.

Author Affiliations: Centre for Research and Advanced Studies, MX; National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM), MX; National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM), MX; National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM), US; Centre for Research and Advanced Studies, MX; University of Campinas, BR; National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM), MX; Universidad Michoacana San Nicolas de Hidalgo, MX; National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM), MX
Publication Title: IEEE Latin America Transactions
Abstract: This paper introduces the real-time implementation with the actual hardware architecture environment (HAE) of an online estimation method that tracks the equivalent time-varying inertia in power systems. The proposed method enables automated and accurate inertia estimation, exploiting the ARMAX model representation and the Teager-Kaiser energy operator (TKEO) disturbance time detector. The effectiveness and high accuracy of the proposed framework are successfully validated in laboratory conditions with actual synchronised measurements from Phasor Measurement Units (PMUs) over a real-time emulated New England 39-bus system. The estimate is achieved with a relative error ranging from 0.1% to 7%, even under noisy conditions and atypical measurement values. The literature reviewed does not report any estimation method that is more accurate than the one proposed in this work.

Abstract: We present the implementation of a Cloud-Computing-based system for leak diagnosis in a water distribution network. Pressure head/flow rate measured in field is stored and processed in a virtual machine to provide a leak diagnosis in two stages: 1) first the leak is detected by comparing the current operating conditions and expected nominal operating conditions obtained from a previously adjusted simulation model, and 2) the leak exact location and its magnitude are determined using a meta-heuristic method. The performance of the proposed system is implemented for an experimental hydraulic system at a laboratory scale. Results demonstrate a good accuracy in the leak diagnosis metrics at a reduced economic and computational cost, demonstrating the potential results from the implementation of a similar system in a large-scale water distribution network.

Document Title: AMIND: An Asynchronous Middleware for Improving Neighborhood Discovery in Crowded Networks
Authors: J. J. Camacho-Escoto; F. García; O. Arana-Hernández; J. Gomez
Author Affiliations: Department of Computing, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Mexico City, Mexico; Department of Telecommunications, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Mexico City, Mexico; Department of Telecommunications, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Mexico City, Mexico; Department of Telecommunications, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Mexico City, Mexico
Publication Title: IEEE Access
Abstract: Neighbor discovery (ND) is crucial in deploying wireless sensor networks. Despite the considerable research on ND, most studies focus on networks composed of only two nodes. However, for cases in which more than two nodes are involved, the probability of collision increases, potentially preventing a node from discovering all its neighbors. This problem is especially aggravated for ND protocols that exhibit periodic behaviors that can lead to recurring collisions. This behavior is typical of deterministic ND protocols, in which its inherent operation forces regular on/off patterns and cyclical collisions. To address this problem, this paper introduces AMIND, a new middleware capable of disrupting the cyclic behavior of deterministic ND protocols. It achieves this goal by introducing random shifts in their operation, significantly increasing the number of nodes discovered compared to the existing ND protocols. As a result, AMIND allows a node to discover unknown neighbors after each shift. Simulation results show that the proposed middleware enhances node discoveries that previous ND protocols can achieve, even in scenarios with high collision rates.

Publication Title: 2024 IEEE 22nd Mediterranean Electrotechnical Conference (MELECON)
Abstract: Low-inertia power systems require more innovative operation, control, and protection strategies to maintain the operation secure and reliable. One of the challenges related to these systems is the need for knowledge of the inertia level (kinetic energy stored in the rotating masses) in real time. This paper proposes a framework for training and deploying machine learning methods for real-time power systems’ kinetic energy forecasting. Linear Regression and Long Short-Term Memory methods are implemented in the Python interpreter of the Typhoon HIL 404 real-time simulator for forecasting the kinetic energy of the Nordic Power System in real-time. This paper provides implementation details together with possible future expansions of the framework. Simulation results show that the trained models can predict the kinetic energy in a forecasting horizon of four hours with a Mean Absolute Error lower than other methods currently available in the literature.

Authors: L. Olvera-Martinez; M. Cedillo-Hernandez; C. Diaz-Rodriguez; L. Faustinos-Morales
Author Affiliations: Instituto Politécnico Nacional Escuela Superior de Ingeniería, Mecánica y Eléctrica Unidad Culhuacan, CDMX, México; Instituto Politécnico Nacional Escuela Superior de Ingeniería, Mecánica y Eléctrica Unidad Culhuacan, CDMX, México; Instituto Politécnico Nacional Escuela Superior de Ingeniería, Mecánica y Eléctrica Unidad Culhuacan, CDMX, México; Universidad Nacional Autónoma de Mexico, CDMX, Mexico
Publication Title: 2025 10th International Conference on Frontiers of Signal Processing (ICFSP)
Abstract: With the growing demand for secure and efficient digital image processing, the integration of data hiding and encryption techniques has become essential to ensure both confidentiality and integrity of visual information. This paper proposes a hybrid approach that combines a low complexity steganographic scheme for binary and color images with a computationally efficient encryption technique inspired by quantum operators. In the data hiding component, an RGB channel of a color image is selected, and its least significant bit plane is extracted. This bit plane is then divided into suitable blocks, and HVS-based encoding tables are used to embed the information within these blocks, achieving high embedding capacity while preserving visual quality. In parallel, the encryption stage operates on color images and is based on a novel operator model that simulates quantum behaviors such as creation, exchange, and annihilation, enabling effective protection without requiring intensive mathematical processing. Experimental validation confirms the robustness of the combined approach through quality metrics such as PSNR, SSIM and entropy.
Author Keywords: Braided Quantum;Quantum Operators;Dynamic Matrices;Finite Fields
IEEE Terms: Visualization;Steganography;Signal processing algorithms;Color;Signal processing;Robustness;Real-time systems;Encryption;Data mining;Protection

Authors: V. Gonzalez; V. Torres-García; D. Guillen; L. M. Castro
Author Affiliations: Postgraduate in Electrical Engineering, National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM), Mexico City, Mexico; GIIE, National Technological Institute of Mexico, Morelia, Mexico; School of Engineering and Sciences, Tecnologico de Monterrey, Monterrey, Nuevo Leon, Mexico; Department of Electrical Energy, National Autonomous University of Mexico (UNAM), Mexico City, Mexico
Publication Title: IEEE Open Access Journal of Power and Energy
Abstract: Fault location has been crucial in minimizing fault restoration time. Various techniques and methodologies have been deployed to enhance the performance of fault location algorithms, especially in light of the increasing integration of renewable energy sources. In this context, this paper describes a graph-theory-based method for fault location in power networks with renewable energy sources. This novel technique is designed to provide accurate fault distance estimates, even in the presence of severe noise and fault resistance. It takes advantage of graph theory and equivalent impedances applying Kirchhoff’s laws systematically to ensure accurate fault location even in the presence of fault resistances. To showcase the improved accuracy of the proposed methodology, a comparison with typical impedance-based two-terminal fault location methods is carried out. The effectiveness of the proposed algorithm was proven with different electrical systems. Average errors inferior to 0.22% and 0.48% were obtained for single-phase faults and three-phase faults with resistances up to  $200~\Omega $  respectively, which confirms the improved performance with respect to conventional algorithms implemented in typical impedance relays.

Author Affiliations: Department of Electrical Engineering, University of Zanjan, Zanjan, Iran; Graduate School of Intelligent Data Science, National Yunlin University of Science and Technology, Douliou, Taiwan; Department of Electrical Engineering, National Kaohsiung University of Science and Technology, Kaohsiung City, Taiwan; Department of Electrical Engineering, University of Zanjan, Zanjan, Iran; Department of Electrical Engineering, University of Zanjan, Zanjan, Iran; Facultad de Ingeniería, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México (UNAM), Mexico City, Mexico; Department of Electrical Engineering, National Kaohsiung University of Science and Technology, Kaohsiung City, Taiwan
Publication Title: IEEE Transactions on Transportation Electrification
Abstract: The magnetic levitation (MAGLEV) train faces challenging conditions, including actuator faults, high-bandwidth disturbances, and parametric uncertainties. This study presents a nonsingular fast terminal sliding mode controller renowned for its robustness and rapid convergence. The barrier function technique is employed to mitigate the adverse effects of high-bandwidth disturbances and to regulate energy consumption. The proposed controller utilizes a hyperbolic tangent function at the sliding surface (SS) and control input to alleviate issues caused by the sign function. Hardware results using the OPAL-RT system and MATLAB simulations authenticate the efficacy of the proposed controller in mitigating actuator faults and high-bandwidth disturbances, enabling the system to accurately follow defined paths with high convergence speed and minimal chattering. In addition, the study compares the performance of this controller with two other methods and presents the comparative results. In conclusion, the nonsingular adaptive barrier fast terminal sliding mode control (SMC), incorporating the hyperbolic tangent function, performs optimally in addressing issues related to actuator faults and high-bandwidth disturbances in the MAGLEV train system.
Author Keywords: Actuator fault;chattering problem;nonlinear function;nonsingular fast terminal sliding mode;robust control

Publication Title: 2025 IEEE 64th Conference on Decision and Control (CDC)
Abstract: This paper presents the fully implicit (backward-Euler) discretization of a PID-like sliding-mode controller for undisturbed double-integrator systems. The method inherits the third-order sliding accuracy of the continuous-time PID-like controller while eliminating discretization chattering. The controller is compared in simulation studies to an explicit Euler discretization and a cascaded implementation with a super-twisting controller in the inner loop. The results reveal unique strengths and weaknesses of each approach. It is finally applied to patient positioning of a real-world magnetic resonance imaging system, demonstrating its practical applicability.

Author Affiliations: CDMX, Universidad Da Vinci, México; CDMX, Universidad Da Vinci, México; Facultad de Estudios Superiores Cuautitlán, Universidad Nacional Autónoma de México, Cuautitlan Izcalli, México
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE Latin American School on Computational Intelligence and Robotics (SAIBOT)
Abstract: Artificial Intelligence (AI), with machine learning (ML) models and deep learning (DL) neural networks, represents a possibility for detecting DoS/DDoS attacks more effectively. The LSTM (Long Short-Term Memory) model is one of the options for detecting these increasingly varied and sophisticated attacks. The objective of this research was to evaluate the application of the LSTM model in the detection of DoS and DDoS cyber attacks on communications networks. This research was designed as a Systematic Literature Review to obtain the most recent studies on the subject. As a result, 86 initial studies were obtained from the databases. Once the entire procedure, including the quality assessment of the studies, was completed, 15 final primary studies were selected. With these primary studies, it was possible to answer all the questions in the review. It should be noted that modern SDN (Software Defined Network) networks, Cloud Computing networks, and classic networks are threatened by this type of attack. The LSTM models applied in the detection of DoS/DDoS attacks can be hybrid or combined. The most commonly used evaluation metrics are Accuracy, Recall, Precision, and F1-Score, and the most frequently found public datasets include NSL-KDD, CICDoS2019, and CICIDS2017. A wide variety of LSTM-based models and approaches that can be used to experiment and compare their performance in terms of communications network security.
Author Keywords: Long short term memory;Systematic literature review;Machine learning;Denial-of-service attack;Network security
IEEE Terms: Measurement;Computational modeling;Denial-of-service attack;NSL-KDD;Market research;Communication networks;Software defined networking;Long short term memory;Cyberattack;Systematic literature review

Authors: A. T. Vié; L. Fridman; R. Galeazzi; D. Papageorgiou
Author Affiliations: Department of Electrical and Photonics Engineering, Technical University of Denmark, Kgs Lyngby, Denmark; Facultad de Ingenieria, Universidad Nacional Autonoma de Mexico, Mexico City, Mexico; Department of Electrical and Photonics Engineering, Technical University of Denmark, Kgs Lyngby, Denmark; Department of Electrical and Photonics Engineering, Technical University of Denmark, Kgs Lyngby, Denmark
Publication Title: 2025 IEEE 64th Conference on Decision and Control (CDC)
Abstract: This article presents an adaptive Super-Twisting Sliding Mode Control framework for uncertain first-order systems, with rate-bounded perturbations, where the bound is constant but unknown. Positive definite barrier functions, when used in self-tuning super-twisting controllers may introduce some conservatism in relation to initial estimations of the perturbation rate bound. Moreover, discrete time implementation of the algorithm does not necessarily guarantee the boundedness of the closed-loop trajectories when sudden changes in the perturbation occur in between two time samples. The salient features of the proposed methodology pertain to extending the use of positive semidefinite barrier functions to Super-Twisting controller adaptation and the employment of a "nested barriers" scheme that ensures boundedness of the solutions even for "unfavourable" perturbations-to-sampling time ratios. The stability of the closed-loop system is assessed via Lyapunov analysis and simulations demonstrate the efficacy of the proposed framework.

Document Title: ODIF: Optimum Duty Cycle Selection Using Indirect Feedback for Neighborhood-Discovery in Crowded Scenarios
Authors: J. J. Camacho-Escoto; J. Gomez; L. Orozco-Barbosa
Author Affiliations: Department of Computer Engineering, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Mexico City, Mexico; Department of Telecommunications Engineering, National Autonomous University of Mexico, Mexico City, Mexico; Albacete Research Institute of Informatics, Universidad de Castilla-La Mancha, Albacete, Spain
Publication Title: IEEE Open Journal of the Communications Society
Abstract: A significant amount of research has focused on minimizing the time it takes for two nodes to discover each other when operating in on-off states to conserve energy, a process known as neighbor discovery. However, less attention has been paid to the related issue of neighborhood-discovery, which involves multiple nodes attempting to find each other. We consider the latter problem to be quite relevant, especially with the increasing popularity of networks made up of multiple IoT devices. This issue is more complex because the likelihood of collisions increases with a greater number of nodes and as the time nodes remain awake (duty cycle) extends. The method we propose, called ODIF, uses indirect feedback (collisions and no-encounters) to estimate the number of nodes and employs a dynamic duty cycle algorithm to minimize either latency or energy expenditure during neighborhood-discovery. We compare ODIF with a randomized discovery protocol (referred to as Plain) and two other similar methods found in the existing literature. It was found that ODIF significantly reduces the neighbor discovery latency (or the expected number of attempts) compared to the baseline randomized neighbor discovery protocol for most values of the duty cycle. Similarly, ODIF achieved neighborhood-discovery 1 to 10 times faster for high node densities compared to similar protocols.

Abstract: Due to the continual growth of electrical grids to meet increasing demand, integrating electronic devices and new generation units has become a common practice within electrical power systems. These modifications in network topology can introduce new vulnerable nodes. Consequently, there is a growing need for robust strategies to accurately quantify and assess the vulnerability of these evolving power systems. Many existing methodologies primarily leverage topological information to enhance the understanding of system vulnerabilities. In response to these challenges, this research introduces a hybrid methodology for detecting and validating vulnerable nodes. This novel approach combines a dedicated vulnerability metric to pinpoint nodes of critical significance with applying transient stability analysis to validate these nodes systematically. Through this innovative approach, this work contributes to the comprehensive understanding of vulnerability within electrical power systems and offers practical insights into enhancing system resilience.

Abstract: This article introduces a novel approach to addressing supply chain and logistics challenges using ramified optimal transport. This framework provides a flexible and efficient means of modeling nonlinear and realistic scenarios within natural and complex transportation networks, particularly in real-time applications where route optimization is critical. The problem considered involves determining optimal warehouse locations and devising distribution strategies for delivering goods from suppliers to customers. To this end, we developed two new estimation algorithms and benchmarked them against traditional and heuristic methods. The proposed algorithms demonstrated competitive performance in solving supply chain and logistics problems, with one of them achieving the best results in terms of computational cost. Finally, the algorithms were applied to real-world data from the cement industry, successfully producing real-time optimal routes. This was made possible by the incorporation of a carefully designed distance function in the cost modeling.

Abstract: In this paper, real time validation of active power injection by solar + wind + batteries microgrids in electrical distribution networks using software in the loop (SIL) is presented. The wind fluctuations and irradiance conditions are analyzed from the peninsular region through the Mexican Energy Atlas project. The variability of environmental conditions that impairs a continuous power production affects the voltage at the PCC. Then, this research proposes a control law for the microgrid output power interconnection into the distributed electrical networks, without voltage fluctuations and satisfying the users electrical demand. The proposed methodology functionality is validated through the simulations in MATLAB-Simulink® and tests based on the SIL methodology with the real-time simulator Opal-RT Technologies®. Abstract: Human-robot interaction is a core pillar in mobile robotics for efficient and diverse product development. This paper provides an advanced web-based control interface for a differential robot which allows full management and real-time monitoring of its behavior. It operates through an ESP32 microcontroller where both sides communicate with a web client using the WebSocket protocol. There are three operating modes: manual by sliders, autonomous navigation toward distinct (X, Y) coordinates, and a pioneering trajectory-tracking mode that lets the operator generate a path directly on a digital canvas. Also, the user interface has a monitoring panel with real-time information on such things as motor RPM, position, and robot orientation, all supported by dynamic charts. Differential kinematics and sensor fusion enable hardware and software integration by merging encoder-based odometry with an IMU for more precise positioning. Document Title: Spectral Characterization of the Ictal Phase in Epileptic Seizures of Temporal Lobe Using the Wavelet Transform
Authors: G. González-González; V. M. Velasco-Herrera; C. Alvarado-Serrano; A. Ortega-Aguilar
Author Affiliations: Biophysics and Medical Physics Department, Faculty of Sciences, UNAM, Mexico City, Mexico; Solar Radiation Department, Institute of Geophysics, UNAM, Mexico City, Mexico; Electrical Engineering Department, Bioelectronics Section, CINVESTAV, Mexico City, Mexico; Biochemistry Department, Faculty of Medicine, UNAM, Mexico City, Mexico
Publication Title: 2025 22nd International Conference on Electrical Engineering, Computing Science and Automatic Control (CCE)
Abstract: Epilepsy is a chronic disorder of brain function related to neuronal hyperactivity and hypersychronization. The electroencephalogram is the main clinical technic used to the epilepsy diagnosis and study. The ictal phase of the epileptic activity occurs when the brain and behavior manifestations are most prominent, however, there are no objective parameters to determine it, as well as to identify its onset and offset. In this research we propose a spectral time-frequency analysis to characterize this phase. Twenty-one ictal phases of temporal lobe epilepsy from men and women with focal onset were analyzed with the wavelet transform. Specific power and frequency patterns on the spectrograms were identified in 75% of ictal phases: an intermittent activity in $\delta$ band, followed by a hypersynchronization in $\delta$ and $\theta$ bands, and finally a power dispersion from 2 to 30 Hz; regardless of onset lateralization and sex. This pattern clearly limited the onset and offset of the seizure. Additionally, power and frequencies dispersion analysis between the nearest channels to the focus and the other channels were performed. No dispersion patterns were observed, but it was proposed that it could be related to the individual brain damage caused by the recurrent seizures. In conclusion, the quantitative analysis of ictal phases by means of wavelet transform offered a new visualization of the ictal phase and highlighted objective information in power and frequency, which can be used for helping with understanding and classification of this kind of seizures.
Author Keywords: Epilepsy;ictal phase;neuronal synchronization;wavelet analysis
IEEE Terms: Temporal lobe;Visualization;Time-frequency analysis;Statistical analysis;Epilepsy;Wavelet analysis;Robustness;Synchronization;Dispersion;Spectrogram

Author Affiliations: UDEE-F1, UNaM, Oberá, Argentina; UDEE-F1, UNaM-CONICET, Oberá, Argentina; GEA-IITEMA, UNRC-CONICET, Río Cuarto, Argentina; FI-UBA, Instituto de Ingenierta Biomedica, Buenos Aires, Argentina
Publication Title: 2025 XXI Workshop on Information Procesing and Control (RPIC)
Abstract: This paper presents a strategy for detecting interturn short-circuit (ITSC) faults in permanent magnet synchronous machines (PMSM) based on tracking the fundamental component of a residual voltage using the Vold-Kalman filter (VKF). The vector models of the voltages at the midpoint of the phase windings for a faulty PMSM are presented. In addition, the orthogonal vector (OV) model of the VKF algorithm used to compute the magnitude and phase shift from the measured voltage is described. Experimental results for a PMSM with ITSC of varying severity under both no-load and full-load conditions are presented. It is demonstrated that a ITSC fault can be reliably detected from 1 % fault severity, and the corresponding faulty winding can be accurately identified.

The proposed PLC-based PS integrates various components such as sensors and actuators, with sensor nodes deployed within parking spaces to detect the presence of vehicles.
